sentences.text: He called her a dork.
Catch.
[THROWING KEYS] I have no pockets.
I'll lose them.
[INAUDIBLE].
Don't have pockets.
Right.
Do I look at you?
Hello.
My name is Gerry, or Gerald, Grossman.
I live in the United States of America now.
And I'm going to give you a little story about my life, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the time of war.
I was born in the city of Rowne under the name Gedalia Tennenbojm.
It was Poland in the district of Volhynia.
My father was David Tennenbojm He was a tailor all his life.
My mother, Tsipa Tennenbojm Her maiden name was Gresores.
And she was a housewife.
We were five children.
I was the oldest one.
My brother, next brother, was born in 1928 in the city of Rowne.
His name was Isar Tennenbojm The next brother was Pesach, or [?
Pesar ?] Tennenbojm, who was born in 1930.
I had a little sister, Feiga was born in 1932.
And then, the youngest brother was born in 1935.
His name was Motel Tennenbojm.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had a pretty nice, not a rich life, but it was interesting.
The city was a-- a population of about 57,000 people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] About 30,000 were Jewish.
Most of the Jews lived in the city.
And the city had a lot of organizations, Zionist organizations, that we belonged to.
I belonged to an organization named [NON-ENGLISH] when I was young.
Mizrahi-- and we had [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], and a lot of other organizations-- all just sort of preparation to go eventually to Israel.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's what the life was all about.
It was very interesting.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a happy life in the city.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I went to school.
And when I was five years old, I started to go to Cheder to learn Yiddish and Hebrew.
When I was seven years old, I started a public school, the Polish public school, which was compulsory at that time.
And when I finished public school, I signed into a special school, [INAUDIBLE] sponsored by American [INAUDIBLE] until the beginning of the war of 1939, when--
I was interrupted.
I was, at the time of the war, 17 years old.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in 1939, when [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the war started with Poland, like Germany and Poland, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Russia made a pact, or agreement, with Germany to divide Poland in half.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the Germans took Poland up to the River Bug, near Lvov and Kovel and Brest-Litovsk, around that area.
And the Russians came in on the other side.
And they took the part where I lived.
So automatically, when the Russians came into us in September of 1939, I was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] already on the Russian camp, sort of saying.
I started to go to Russian schools.
Was that mandatory?
No.
I wanted to learned the Russian language.
Although in my young age, I spoke Ukrainian, because I used to play with Ukrainian friends.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so it was easier for me to learn the language of Russian because it's similar.
There was anti-Semitism at all times in Poland and in my area.
But we were sort of used to that.
We accepted it because we felt that we don't live in our own country.
Well, we had a lot of problems.

And anti-Semitism was part of that part of Rowne because Rowne before the first World War belonged to Russia and all the pogroms that happened during the Russian-- the Russian, that Czar that was there before the revolution.
So sort of, the Jews accepted it.
And that's why we always wanted to have our children sort of go to Israel and build their own country.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had to live with the situation like that.
During holidays, like Christmas and New Year's, most of the Jews would be sitting in the houses, not go out because after the-- the-- the masses, church, people would come out.
They would so worked out that they would be hitting or beating up the Jews for something that happened 2,000 years ago.
Yes.
My-- my-- well, sort of observant.
We were celebrating Shabbos, which is Saturday.
My father used to come home because he always used to work in Obariv.
It's a little town out of the city.
He was a tailor among the Ukrainian people.
There was a little town that just Ukrainian people lived there.
And they liked us very much.
We-- I used to go there and play with the Ukrainian people, speak to them in their language.
And we were sort of friends.
In the city of Rowne, it was a little different.
There, you mixed among your own Jewish people.
But I was very happy to be in that little town, Obariv.
And we had friends.
My father had friends.
And therefore, for Saturday, he used to come home.
So he was away all week.
And Saturday or Friday night, he used to come home and leave on Sunday again for the week to work.
And we used to go to synagogue [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and be with the family.
My father used to play the violin, I remember, and sort of left all the five children with my mother.
And she had to care for us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was life before the war.
Well, when the war started-- 1939, September 1939, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was a big change.
Just before the war started, we heard all the speeches that Hitler used to give.
And there was unrest in the cities all around us.
We didn't have a radio.
And we used to gather in some houses where they had a radio just to listen to his speeches.
And we knew that something bad is coming, but we couldn't understand it.

[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We started-- the government, the Polish government-- started to build trenches, get ready for the war because Hitler was threatening the Polish government with all kinds of things.
As a matter of fact, I remember a case when just before the war, before 1939, there was a Madame Pristerover, which brought in a law not to have kosher-- kosher meat in Poland.
And they restricted of the killing of cows and chickens in the kosher ways.
That means in the slaughterhouses.
And I remember when the Jews didn't eat meat for-- for months just because of that.
And they were trying to do things that should interfere with the internal politics of Poland just to make trouble.

When the war started in '39 and Russia took a part of Poland and most of the [NON-ENGLISH] they called them at the time, from the Polish side-- from the German side of Poland were running towards our city of Rowne and Lutsk and other cities in Volhynia where I remember we put them up--
--in the houses.
Jews that were running away.
They were asked by the Russians what they want to do, whether they want to go back to Germany to their part of Poland, or would they like to go to Russia?
And most of them said, yes.
We would like to go back home.
And just because of that, the Russian government sent them away one day to Siberia.
My personal experiences-- in 1939, when the Russians came in, I learned the language of Russian.
And then, they opened up a school, which was called [NON-ENGLISH].
In Russian, it's called [RUSSIAN], where you learn a trade while you're learning other things.
It's like a trade school.
After six months, I graduated that school.
I learned, in the town of Zdolbuniv, which is 12 kilometers from Rowne, to work on a locomotive.
They call it locomotive engineering.
And on the railroad, other things, whatever is involved for the railroad.
When I graduated sometime in 1941-- I'm sorry, that was in 1940-- I was sent to a town, which was called Kamionka Strumilowa.
The town is about 28 kilometers from Lvov on the border-- the new border-- of Germany.
And I worked on that station until the war started in 1941, June the  22nd.
Well, actually the war-- the war was started in '39.
But in '41 is when the Germans attacked Russia.
I was on that station working-- 4 o'clock in the morning on a Sunday, the 22nd of June.
I saw on the station over the-- over our railroad station, I saw German planes fighting with Russians already.
But we still didn't know that Germany attacked Russia.
All we knew at our station is that the Germans already broke through the lines.
Like in Lutsk was the next station that the trains used to come to our station.
They bombarded the railroad.
They bombarded Kovel.
And they crossed the border.
About 12 o'clock, noon on Sunday, that day, Molotov, the foreign minister of Russia, announced that Germany attacked Russia.
And the war between Russia and Germany started.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The-- what happened at that station that I was working that day, and my chief, which was Russian, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] told me that he wants to take his family to Lvov, away from the border.
And-- and then, he's going to come back.
In the meantime, no trains were coming to our station because the railroad was completely destroyed.
I didn't feel so good to stay all by myself behind.
So I asked him whether it's OK for me to help him to take his family to Lvov.
And then, we will come back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He agreed.
And it was my luck because we had only one locomotive left on our station.
And when we went out of the station, we could never come back anymore.
The bombardment over transport that we were traveling and the shooting over us-- just couldn't come back.
We never made it to Lvov, but we avoided, and we started to travel more east.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It took three days for me to come to the city of Zdolbuniv.
That's 12 kilometers from Rowne.
Because while we were traveling, the German planes used to come and bombard our transport.
But before when we heard the planes running, we used to stop and disperse until the planes went away.
Traveling by train to come to Zdolbuniv to run away from the area of the-- of the invasion.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When I came back to Zdolbuniv, which was only 12 kilometers from the city of Rowne, I decided to go and visit my family.
I walked the 12 kilometers to Rowne.
I met my family.
And that was already, I think, June the 25th, 1941.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I saw the whole family, but in the meantime, the city was being bombarded constantly.
I asked them to move from one part of the city to another part of the city, the other end, to hide.
Maybe it's more safer over there.
Well, I took them over there.
But I had to go back to work.
I was assigned a job for 10 o'clock at night to work on the station of Zdolbuniv.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I had to appear for that time.
About 8 o'clock in the evening, I said to my mother that I have to leave.
And I'll see you back tomorrow, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because I have to be on the job.
If I won't show up and something happens in Russia, they could send you away to Siberia for that or other things.
So I said to my mother, I'm leaving.
And I'll see you tomorrow.
She says, don't go, my son.
Stay with us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then, she started to cry because I'm leaving.
The city's being bombarded.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I spoke to my father.
And I told him that mother wants me to stay, and I can't do it.
I remember his words that he told me.
He said, son, you're now a grown man.
You have to make the decision what you have to do.
And I can't tell you better to stay.
I would like to have you here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I knew that I have to go back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said goodbye to my whole family.
And this was the last time I saw my family, because I never had another chance to come back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother was crying.
And I walked away without turning my head because I had tears in my eyes.
I didn't want to show them that I'm a boy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I walked about six miles from the city [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the railroad tracks, when I saw a whole group of German planes just flying over my head.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were very low.
And I saw them going towards the city of Rowne.
And in a few seconds later, they were there.
They spread themselves all through the whole city, dropped their bombs, and put the whole city on fire.
And I was standing just halfway [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the city and from my place of work.
I couldn't make up my mind what to do.
I didn't know whether I should run back and see what happened to my family because at 8 o'clock at night, it was already dark.
You could see the whole city on fire.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I decided to go back to do-- to my work to Zdolbuniv.
I figured in the morning, the first thing after my job is finished, I will run back to the city and see what happened to my family.
Well, it didn't happen that way, because when I came to work, I was on the station.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In-- after 10 o'clock, about 11 o'clock, they German planes came again and started to bombard that big station that was there.
There were soldiers up there.
And there was ammunition on the-- on the station.
Russian soldiers.
And they tried to knock it out.
Well, while I was there in between all the cars and the locomotives and the planes, I-- I thought, it's not a good idea to stay here.
I went out from this station.
I ran out.
And I walked towards the outskirts of Zdolbuniv.
Being that I was very tired and didn't sleep for the last three days coming from Lvov, I walked into one of the houses there-- it was mostly Gentile people lived there.
And I asked if it's OK for me to lie down and sleep there until the morning.
And then, would you please wake me up in the morning, very early in the morning, so I could get out and run back to work?
She said, OK.
There are already some men there sleeping on the floor.
And if you want to lie down on the floor, it's OK.
So I did.
I fell asleep.
And she claims she couldn't wake me up early in the morning because that's how tired I was.
I walked out.
It was already daylight.
And I ran back towards the station to see what happened over there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as I was walking, right next to rails-- railroad over there to the station, I saw a German tank pointing the gun towards a group of Russian soldiers with their hands up.
And I was walking right next to the tank, not realizing what's going on, with the uniform of the railroad I was wearing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And while they were busy with the soldiers, I went behind the tank.
I ran up to the railroad.
And I looked over there.
Nobody was there already.
They evacuated in the middle of the night most of the locomotives and cars that was stationed over there.
I was confused.
I didn't know what to do.
So I ran, and I found my suitcase that I had in one of the warehouses.
And I ran towards the smoke that I saw further up and-- that the locomotives-- I saw them leaving slowly.
So I ran towards them, where one of my friends that was also going to the same school recognized me from far.
He slowed down the locomotive.
And I jumped on it.
